title: Frank Vincent

infobox.value: Frank Vincent Gattuso Jr.  (1937-04-15)April 15, 1937  [North Adams, Massachusetts](/wiki/North_Adams,_Massachusetts "North Adams, Massachusetts"), U.S.
September 13, 2017(2017-09-13) (aged 80)  [Nutley, New Jersey](/wiki/Nutley,_New_Jersey "Nutley, New Jersey"), U.S.

(April 15, 1937 – September 13, 2017)[[1]](#cite_note-huffpost-1) was an American actor.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Known for often portraying violent mobsters and criminals, he was a frequent collaborator of filmmaker [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"), appearing as Salvy in *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), [Billy Batts](/wiki/Billy_Batts "Billy Batts") in *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), and Frank Marino in *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995).[[3]](#cite_note-Post-3) On television, he played [Phil Leotardo](/wiki/Phil_Leotardo "Phil Leotardo") on the [fifth](/wiki/The_Sopranos_season_5 "The Sopranos season 5") and [sixth](/wiki/The_Sopranos_season_6 "The Sopranos season 6") seasons of the [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") crime drama *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")* (2004–2007). Vincent voiced Salvatore Leone in the *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")* video game series from 2001 to 2005. Vincent also worked as an acting coach, providing assistance and guidance to co-stars who did not have formal acting training.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor (1937–2017)

For other people named Frank Vincent, see [Frank Vincent (disambiguation)](/wiki/Frank_Vincent_(disambiguation) "Frank Vincent (disambiguation)").

| Frank Vincent | |
| --- | --- |
| Vincent in 2012 | |
| Born | Frank Vincent Gattuso Jr.  (1937-04-15)April 15, 1937  [North Adams, Massachusetts](/wiki/North_Adams,_Massachusetts "North Adams, Massachusetts"), U.S. |
| Died | September 13, 2017(2017-09-13) (aged 80)  [Nutley, New Jersey](/wiki/Nutley,_New_Jersey "Nutley, New Jersey"), U.S. |
| Occupation | Actor |
| Years active | 1970–2017 |
| Spouse | Kathleen Vincent  ​ (m. 1970)​ |
| Children | 3 |

**Frank Vincent Gattuso Jr.** (April 15, 1937 – September 13, 2017)[[1]](#cite_note-huffpost-1) was an American actor.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Known for often portraying violent mobsters and criminals, he was a frequent collaborator of filmmaker [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"), appearing as Salvy in *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), [Billy Batts](/wiki/Billy_Batts "Billy Batts") in *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), and Frank Marino in *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995).[[3]](#cite_note-Post-3) On television, he played [Phil Leotardo](/wiki/Phil_Leotardo "Phil Leotardo") on the [fifth](/wiki/The_Sopranos_season_5 "The Sopranos season 5") and [sixth](/wiki/The_Sopranos_season_6 "The Sopranos season 6") seasons of the [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") crime drama *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")* (2004–2007). Vincent voiced Salvatore Leone in the *[Grand Theft Auto](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto "Grand Theft Auto")* video game series from 2001 to 2005. Vincent also worked as an acting coach, providing assistance and guidance to co-stars who did not have formal acting training.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Vincent&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Vincent, who was of [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") descent with roots in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") and [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), was born in [North Adams, Massachusetts](/wiki/North_Adams,_Massachusetts "North Adams, Massachusetts"), and raised in [Jersey City, New Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey").[[6]](#cite_note-6) His father, Frank Vincent Gattuso Sr., was an [iron worker](/wiki/Iron_worker "Iron worker") and businessman.[[7]](#cite_note-FRB-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) He had two brothers, Nick and Jimmy, and a half-sister, Fran.[[7]](#cite_note-FRB-7)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Vincent&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Skilled at the drums, piano and trumpet, Vincent originally aspired to a career in music. By day he was a [studio musician](/wiki/Studio_musician "Studio musician") who worked with many recording artists such as [Paul Anka](/wiki/Paul_Anka "Paul Anka") and [Del Shannon](/wiki/Del_Shannon "Del Shannon"). Vincent had his own jazz band, billed "Frank Vincent and the Aristocats", that played in the evenings. In 1969, Vincent's band needed a piano player to secure bookings and Vincent ended up hiring [Joe Pesci](/wiki/Joe_Pesci "Joe Pesci") to play guitar. As the popularity of [lounge music](/wiki/Lounge_music "Lounge music") waned, Vincent and Pesci turned to [stand-up comedy](/wiki/Stand-up_comedy "Stand-up comedy"), performing as "Vincent and Pesci" from 1970 to 1976. Their act coupled [Abbott and Costello](/wiki/Abbott_and_Costello "Abbott and Costello")-inspired [double-act](/wiki/Double_act "Double act") antics with [Don Rickles](/wiki/Don_Rickles "Don Rickles")-style [insult comedy](/wiki/Insult_comedy "Insult comedy"), which proved popular. During this time, the men developed a strong professional and personal friendship.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Vincent and Pesci later landed parts in the low-budget gangster film *[The Death Collector](/wiki/The_Death_Collector "The Death Collector")* (1976), where they were spotted by [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro"). De Niro told [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese") about both Vincent and Pesci. Scorsese was impressed by their performances and hired Vincent to appear in a supporting role in *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980), in which he once again appeared with Pesci and co-starred with De Niro.[[11]](#cite_note-People-11) Vincent appeared in small roles in two [Spike Lee](/wiki/Spike_Lee "Spike Lee") films: *[Do the Right Thing](/wiki/Do_the_Right_Thing "Do the Right Thing")* (1989) and *[Jungle Fever](/wiki/Jungle_Fever "Jungle Fever")* (1991). In the latter, he played the abusive patriarch of an Italian-American family.[[12]](#cite_note-Variety-12)

One of his notable appearances in foreign films was as a supporting character in [Juan José Jusid](/wiki/Juan_Jos%C3%A9_Jusid "Juan José Jusid")'s *[Made in Argentina](/wiki/Made_in_Argentina "Made in Argentina")* (1987), in which he played Vito, a wealthy Manhattan businessman who befriends the film's protagonist, a substance-abuse counselor who treated Vito's son, played by [Luis Brandoni](/wiki/Luis_Brandoni "Luis Brandoni").[[13]](#cite_note-USAT-13)

Vincent was often cast as a [gangster](/wiki/Gangster "Gangster").[[13]](#cite_note-USAT-13) For example, in Scorsese's film *[Goodfellas](/wiki/Goodfellas "Goodfellas")* (1990), he played [Billy Batts](/wiki/Billy_Batts "Billy Batts"), a [made man](/wiki/Made_man "Made man") in the [Gambino crime family](/wiki/Gambino_crime_family "Gambino crime family"), who is killed by Joe Pesci's character;[[14]](#cite_note-THR-14) he also played a role in Scorsese's film *[Casino](/wiki/Casino_(1995_film) "Casino (1995 film)")* (1995) as Frank Marino (based on real-life gangster [Frank Cullotta](/wiki/Frank_Cullotta "Frank Cullotta")), the sidekick of Pesci's character who ends up killing him.[[15]](#cite_note-TCST-15)

In 1996, Vincent appeared in the music video for rap artist [Nas](/wiki/Nas "Nas")' song "[Street Dreams](/wiki/Street_Dreams_(song) "Street Dreams (song)")."[[11]](#cite_note-People-11) In the television movie *[Gotti](/wiki/Gotti_(1996_film) "Gotti (1996 film)")* (1996), Vincent played [Robert "D.B." DiBernardo](/wiki/Robert_DiBernardo "Robert DiBernardo"), an associate of Mafia boss [John Gotti](/wiki/John_Gotti "John Gotti")'s, whose life the film chronicled.[[12]](#cite_note-Variety-12) Vincent appeared in a small role in the 1998 film *[Belly](/wiki/Belly_(film) "Belly (film)")*, starring Nas and [DMX](/wiki/DMX "DMX") and directed by [Hype Williams](/wiki/Hype_Williams "Hype Williams"). Vincent also served as an acting coach on the film.[[16]](#cite_note-16) In the [HBO](/wiki/HBO "HBO") TV series *[The Sopranos](/wiki/The_Sopranos "The Sopranos")*, he had his most prominent role as [Phil Leotardo](/wiki/Phil_Leotardo "Phil Leotardo"), a ruthless New York City gangster who, as [boss](/wiki/Crime_boss "Crime boss") of the show's fictional [Lupertazzi crime family](/wiki/Lupertazzi_crime_family "Lupertazzi crime family"), becomes the show's chief antagonist in the final season.[[17]](#cite_note-EOnline-17)

In 1999, Vincent won the Italian American Entertainer of the Year Award.[[11]](#cite_note-People-11)

In 2003, Vincent appeared in the film *[Remedy](/wiki/Remedy_(film) "Remedy (film)")*. That same year, Vincent portrayed Danny Santini in the film *[This Thing of Ours](/wiki/This_Thing_of_Ours_(film) "This Thing of Ours (film)")*, whose associate producer was [Sonny Franzese](/wiki/Sonny_Franzese "Sonny Franzese"), longtime mobster and [underboss](/wiki/Underboss "Underboss") of the [Colombo crime family](/wiki/Colombo_crime_family "Colombo crime family"), and whose director was [Genovese crime family](/wiki/Genovese_crime_family "Genovese crime family") capo Danny Provenzano (grandnephew of [Anthony Provenzano](/wiki/Anthony_Provenzano "Anthony Provenzano")).[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Later that year, Vincent testified in court on the behalf of Provenzano at repeal sentences; Provenzano was serving a 10-year sentence for [racketeering](/wiki/Racketeering "Racketeering") and other charges.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

One of Vincent's lighter-hearted roles was in a British television commercial for [Peugeot](/wiki/Peugeot "Peugeot") cars.[[17]](#cite_note-EOnline-17) In early 2005, he appeared on Irish television in a series of commercials for Irish bank [Permanent TSB](/wiki/Permanent_TSB "Permanent TSB").[[14]](#cite_note-THR-14)

In video games, Vincent voiced the character of Mafia boss [Salvatore Leone](/wiki/Salvatore_Leone "Salvatore Leone") in the [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") *[Grand Theft Auto III](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto_III "Grand Theft Auto III")* (2001). He later reprised the role in *[Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto:_San_Andreas "Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas")* (2004) and *[Grand Theft Auto: Liberty City Stories](/wiki/Grand_Theft_Auto:_Liberty_City_Stories "Grand Theft Auto: Liberty City Stories")* (2005).[[14]](#cite_note-THR-14)

In 2006 he released a book, *A Guy's Guide to Being a Man's Man,* to positive reviews.[[15]](#cite_note-TCST-15) His idol was [Dean Martin](/wiki/Dean_Martin "Dean Martin").[[22]](#cite_note-22) He has also released a line of hand-rolled cigars which have his picture prominently displayed on the band.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

He played Lieutenant Marino in the independent film *[The Tested](/wiki/The_Tested "The Tested")* (2008), directed by Russell Costanzo.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The following year, he made a cameo appearance alongside fellow *Sopranos* actor [Steve Schirripa](/wiki/Steve_Schirripa "Steve Schirripa") in the *[Stargate Atlantis](/wiki/Stargate_Atlantis "Stargate Atlantis")* episode "[Vegas](/wiki/Vegas_(Stargate_Atlantis) "Vegas (Stargate Atlantis)")" (2009).[[25]](#cite_note-25) The following year, he starred in *[Chicago Overcoat](/wiki/Chicago_Overcoat "Chicago Overcoat")* (2009) as the main protagonist.[[15]](#cite_note-TCST-15)

In 2013, he starred in the hit IDW Publishing comic series *Killogy*[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) created by *[Life of Agony](/wiki/Life_of_Agony "Life of Agony")*'s [Alan Robert](/wiki/Alan_Robert "Alan Robert") as the character Sally Sno Cones alongside [Marky Ramone](/wiki/Marky_Ramone "Marky Ramone") of [The Ramones](/wiki/The_Ramones "The Ramones"). The series was nominated at the Ghastly Awards for Best Mini-Series and won multiple Horror Comic Awards from the Horror News Network. In 2014, the comics were adapted into 3D-animation for the *Killogy* animated series,[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) in which the cast of the original comic series contributed their voices.

A resident of [Nutley, New Jersey](/wiki/Nutley,_New_Jersey "Nutley, New Jersey"), Vincent used his drumming skills in an impromptu performance at a township holiday concert.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Vincent&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Death")]

In early September 2017, Vincent suffered a [heart attack](/wiki/Heart_attack "Heart attack").[[31]](#cite_note-31) He underwent [open-heart surgery](/wiki/Open_heart_surgery "Open heart surgery") in New Jersey on September 13; however, he died shortly thereafter, at the age of 80.[[32]](#cite_note-usatoday-32) Director John Gallagher, who worked with Vincent on *Street Hunter* and *The Deli*, noted that the actor lied about his age to avoid age discrimination, and therefore many sources listed his birth year as 1939.[[33]](#cite_note-nytimes-33)

Vincent's remains were cremated at a funeral home in [Montclair, New Jersey](/wiki/Montclair,_New_Jersey "Montclair, New Jersey"), and a funeral service was held on September 16.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Filmography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Vincent&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Filmography")]

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frank_Vincent&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Film")]

| Year | Title | Role | Notes |
